texto: Looking back on it now, I'd say one's thirties are a cruel age. At this point, I think of them as a time I whiled away unaware of the tide that can suddenly pull you out, beyond the shallows, into the sea of hardship, and even death
explicacao: Citação de Takashi Hiraide sobre passagem do tempo e prioridades. “Looking back on it now, I'd say one's thirties are a cruel age. At this point, I think of them as…” articula reflexao, aging, fate em registro de reflexao, com relevância para contextos como luto e fate.

texto: Having played to her heart's content, Chibi would come inside and rest for a while. When she began to sleep on the sofa--like a talisman curled gently in the shape of a comma and dug up from a prehistoric archaeological site--a deep sense of happiness arrived, as if the house itself had dreamed this scene.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Takashi Hiraide: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de vínculos afetivos e cuidado mútuo. O trecho “Having played to her heart's content, Chibi would come inside and rest for a while. When she bega…” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que motivacao, cats, reflexao pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de reflexao.

texto: I wonder where it all comes from--this need to go to the place where the body has been laid to rest. It's the need to reconfirm how precious someone was and how irreplaceable, and the desire to reconnect with them on a different plane.
explicacao: Citação de Takashi Hiraide sobre temas ligados a reflexao. “I wonder where it all comes from--this need to go to the place where the body has been laid to re…” articula reflexao, death, grief em registro de reflexao, com relevância para contextos como luto e grief.

texto: he imagined fate as a goddess, capricious and fickle, or as a river, which could flood at any moment
